summary: Fowler addresses the common concern about agile projects producing less documentation for team handovers. He argues that traditional processes often produce unhelpful documentation that becomes outdated under deadline pressure, while agile favors smaller amounts of higher-quality documentation. The recommended approach is face-to-face knowledge transfer by having support team members work alongside the development team before handover. Having operations staff participate during development also improves relationships and produces better, demand-driven documentation. Automated tests serve as an important additional form of documentation.
key_points: Mandatory documentation from plan-driven processes is often unhelpful and quickly outdated under deadline pressure
Face-to-face knowledge transfer through team overlap is more effective than documentation-heavy handovers
Bringing operations staff onto the development team improves both documentation quality and deployment relationships
Documentation written on-demand for a real audience produces better results than process-mandated documentation
Automated tests constitute an important form of living documentation
money_quotes: I've noticed that processes that produce mandatory documentation often produce stuff that isn't very helpful, and under the pressure of deadlines tends not to be kept up to date.
In many ways the agile approach is a preference for a smaller amount of higher quality documentation.
Communications between development and operations are often strained; frequently the needs of operations are ignored by developers.
They found the documentation very helpful because it was written on demand rather than according to a process.
Quality triumphed over the usual quantity.
XP's huge body of automated tests which - as XPers never tire of pointing out - is an important form of documentation in its own right.
key_insight: Effective knowledge transfer in agile comes from face-to-face collaboration and demand-driven documentation rather than comprehensive but stale process-mandated artifacts.
